steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must join two independent clauses. Left of the blank: "That the geographic center of North America lay in the state of North Dakota was conceded by all [blank]" — the subject is the 'That…' noun clause and the verb is 'was conceded.' Right of the blank: "establishing its precise coordinates proved more divisive" — the gerund phrase 'establishing…' serves as the subject with 'proved' as the verb. Both sides are independent clauses, so they need a punctuation mark that can separate two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — involved: No punctuation between two independent clauses — creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — involved:: A colon must be followed by an explanation, list, or elaboration of what came before — the second clause is a new independent statement, not an elaboration. ✗
  • Choice C — involved;: A semicolon correctly separates two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. ✓
  • Choice D — involved,: A comma alone between two independent clauses creates a comma splice. ✗
C is correct.
